ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salmon donburi; mapped to broader rice bowl dish concept FOODON_00002809.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish with fish — pan-fried salmon on steamed rice (donburi style); mapped to composite rice-and-fish dish category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for salmon donburi; A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for salmon donburi or equivalent rice bowl dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 120–130 g of pan-fried salmon fillet, garnished with spring onions and a light soy-sesame dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Salmon don (pan-fried) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05100118 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g macros are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked salmon and white rice composite (USDA NDB 15236 and 20444) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salmon donburi as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (Jasmine/short-grain type), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The salmon and soy-sesame components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 27.6 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 13.8 per 100 g equivalent serving portion used for GL estimate at 350 g serving: GL = 72 × (27.6 × 3.5 / 100) = 72 × 0.966 = 69.6 / 5 ≈ 13.8 per 100 g basis reported here.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 125 mg per 100 g — well below the 200 mg threshold for moderate concern
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low at 125 mg per 100 g; however, restaurant preparations may add substantially more soy sauce, potentially raising sodium to 300–500 mg per 100 g, so patients with hypertension should request reduced or no soy sauce and avoid additional seasoning at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~72) driven by white rice with very low dietary fibre (0.76 g per 100 g), producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries a GI of approximately 72 and dietary fibre is only 0.76 g per 100 g, meaning this dish will cause a rapid and sustained postprandial glucose rise; patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute brown rice or mixed-grain rice, increase the salmon-to-rice ratio, and pair the meal with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pan-fried salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids with low saturated fat (~0.8 g per 100 g), supporting a cardioprotective lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that reduce triglycerides and support endothelial function; the low saturated fat content (~0.8 g per 100 g) and absence of trans fat make this dish suitable for cardiovascular disease management, provided soy sauce is used sparingly to keep sodium within daily limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 EPA/DHA content from pan-fried salmon with low saturated fat supports anti-inflammatory status
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pan-fried salmon is a primary dietary source of EPA and DHA, which directly attenuate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; patients should request reduced soy sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue oedema and balance the white rice portion with additional salmon to limit refined carbohydrate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salmon don (pan-fried) is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish consisting of a generous portion of pan-fried Atlantic or Pacific salmon fillet served over a bed of steamed white Japanese short-grain rice, typically seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and garnished with sliced spring onions or sesame seeds. The dish is widely served in Japanese casual dining restaurants and food courts across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The salmon is pan-seared to achieve a lightly caramelised exterior while retaining a moist, flaky interior, delivering a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a subtle nuttiness from sesame oil. Nutritionally, the dish provides 167 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (6.69 g), low fat (3.55 g), and a carbohydrate-dominant macronutrient profile (27.6 g) driven by the white rice base. Dietary fibre is low at 0.76 g per 100 g, and sodium is moderate at 125 mg per 100 g under standard preparation. The salmon component contributes clinically meaningful omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making portion control and rice substitution important considerations for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. The dish is dairy-free and can be prepared halal. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore hawker centres, Japanese restaurant chains, and food courts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salmon don?
Is salmon rice bowl good for diabetes?
Is pan-fried salmon don high in sodium?
Can I eat salmon don if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100118, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked salmon and white rice composites, which is an approved secondary source. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (1.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g/100 g), potassium (155 mg/100 g), and cholesterol (18 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked Atlantic salmon (NDB 15236) and cooked white short-grain rice (NDB 20444) in approximate proportions consistent with the HPB-reported macros; these values are not directly reported in the HPB source. 2. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database and a reliable composite estimate from USDA SR Legacy for this exact dish formulation is not available with sufficient precision to report as a supported value. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (Jasmine/short-grain) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). No direct GI measurement for salmon donburi as a composite dish was found in any approved source. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): pan-fried salmon and white rice contain no industrially produced trans fats; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for these ingredients. 5. Halal status is conditional on the soy sauce used being halal-certified; standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) may contain trace alcohol from fermentation.
